verses.fhb_text: Now the serpent was more crafty than any of the wild creatures our Father had made. He said to the woman, "Did God really say, 'You must not eat from any tree in the garden'?"
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The serpent's first words reframe our Father as a distant "God" — FHB's foundational counter-lie marker.

Renderings:
• "our Father" for narrator's YHWH Elohim — per FHB-1.2-1 default
• "God" for the serpent's quoted Elohim — per FHB-1.2-1 speaker-context exception (non-divine speaker, generic predicative usage)
• "wild creatures" for chayyat hassadeh — per FHB-4.5.2-1 (animals get "creature")

Hebrew/Greek: arum (shrewd, cunning) → "more crafty"
verses.footnote: Having rejected our Father, the serpent reframes him as a distant, withholding deity — the foundational lie that hides the Father's heart from his children.

verses.fhb_text: The woman said to the serpent, "We may eat fruit from the trees of the garden,"
verses.decision_notes: Simple narrative — Eve's reply opens her exchange with the serpent.

verses.fhb_text: "but God said, 'You must not eat fruit from the tree that is in the middle of the garden, and you must not touch it, or you will die.'"
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Eve quotes "Elohim" — already inside the serpent's distancing frame.

Renderings:
• "God" for Eve's quoted Elohim — per FHB-1.2-1 speaker-context exception (non-divine speaker, repeating within the serpent's reframe)

verses.fhb_text: "You will certainly not die," the serpent said to the woman.
verses.decision_notes: Simple dialogue — the serpent's flat denial of the warning.

verses.fhb_text: "For God knows that on the day you eat from it your eyes will be opened, and you will be like God, knowing good and evil."
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The serpent's predicative "ke'Elohim" — quality-descriptive, speaker exception governs both occurrences.

Renderings:
• "God" for both occurrences of Elohim — per FHB-1.2-1 speaker-context exception (serpent speaking generically and predicatively, not pointing at the Father)
verses.footnote: The serpent has been lying since Genesis 3:1 that God is a distant, unrelational deity — not the intimate Father he truly is.

verses.fhb_text: When the woman saw that the tree was good for food, pleasing to the eyes, and desirable for gaining wisdom, she took some of its fruit and ate it. She also gave some to her husband, who was with her, and he ate.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The Fall — humanity chooses the serpent's lie over the Father's word.

Renderings:
• "desirable for gaining wisdom" for nechmad ha'etz l'haskil — modern English per FHB-9.9-1

verses.fhb_text: Then the eyes of both of them were opened, and they realized they were naked. So they sewed fig leaves together and made coverings for themselves.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Eyes opened to shame; humanity's first self-covering attempt.

Renderings:
• "realized they were naked" — modern phrasing per FHB-9.9-1
• "coverings" for chagorot — NIV/NLT/ESV consensus per FHB-8.4-1

verses.fhb_text: Then they heard the sound of our Father walking in the garden in the cool of the day, and Adam and his wife hid from our Father among the trees of the garden.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: First Father-child estrangement — humanity hiding from the Father whose presence used to be home.

Renderings:
• "our Father" twice for YHWH Elohim — per FHB-1.2-1 default
• "Adam" for ha'adam — proper name established at Gen 2:20 onward, per FHB-9.11-1
• "cool of the day" for ruach hayyom — NIV/NLT/ESV consensus per FHB-8.4-1

verses.fhb_text: But our Father called to Adam, "Where are you?"
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The Father's first question after the Fall — not for information, but for relationship.

Renderings:
• "our Father" for YHWH Elohim — per FHB-1.2-1
• "Adam" for ha'adam — proper name per FHB-9.11-1

verses.fhb_text: He replied, "I heard you in the garden, and I was afraid because I was naked; so I hid."
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Adam's first response to the Father after the Fall — fear, shame, hiding.

Renderings:
• "He replied" — per FHB-9.14-1 (default to "replied"/"said"; "answered" reserved for emphasis)
• "I heard you in the garden" — modern English per FHB-7.12-1 (drop "the voice of" calque)

verses.fhb_text: And he said, "Who told you that you were naked? Have you eaten from the tree I commanded you not to eat from?"
verses.decision_notes: Simple dialogue — the Father's two questions probe Adam's awareness and obedience.

verses.fhb_text: Adam said, "The woman you put here with me — she gave me some fruit from the tree, and I ate it."
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Adam's deflection — blames both the woman and the Father who gave her.

Renderings:
• "Adam" for ha'adam — proper name per FHB-9.11-1
• Em dash earns its place — sets the woman as appositional emphasis before Adam shifts the blame, per FHB-9.8-1

verses.fhb_text: Then our Father said to the woman, "What is this you have done?" The woman said, "The serpent deceived me, and I ate."
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The Father turns to the woman; she names the serpent's deception.

Renderings:
• "our Father" for YHWH Elohim — per FHB-1.2-1
• "deceived me" for hishi'ani — modern English per FHB-9.9-1

verses.fhb_text: So our Father said to the serpent, "Because you have done this, cursed are you above all livestock and all the wild creatures! You will crawl on your belly and eat dust all the days of your life."
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: First curse — addressed to the serpent who began the lie.

Renderings:
• "our Father" for YHWH Elohim — per FHB-1.2-1
• "livestock" for behemah — NIV/NLT/ESV consensus per FHB-8.4-1
• "wild creatures" for chayyat hassadeh — per FHB-4.5.2-1
• "crawl on your belly" — modern English per FHB-9.9-1
• Exclamation mark earns its place — "cursed are you" is a pronouncement of judgment, exclamatory in force per FHB-9.15-1

verses.fhb_text: And I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your offspring and hers; he will strike your head, and you will strike his heel.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: First messianic promise — the Father's plan of redemption begins inside the curse.

Renderings:
• "offspring" for zera — singular "he" governs (messianic referent), so retain "offspring" rather than the warmer collective "children" per FHB-5.10-1
• "strike... strike" for shuph...shuph — NLT-lean per FHB-8.4-1 (1-1-1 split: NIV crush/strike, NLT strike/strike, ESV bruise/bruise)
• Semicolon preserves Hebrew parallelism between the two clauses per FHB-9.4-2
verses.footnote: From the very first promise after the fall, our Father pointed to Jesus — the offspring who would crush the serpent and rescue his children.

verses.fhb_text: To the woman he said, "I will greatly increase your pain in childbearing; with painful labor you will give birth to children. You will desire to control your husband, but he will rule over you."
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Judgment on the woman — pain in childbearing, marital tension.

Renderings:
• "I will greatly increase your pain in childbearing" — compresses harba arbeh itzvonek v'heronek (pain + conception) into one modern English phrase
• "you will desire to control your husband" — NLT-lean per FHB-8.4-1 (1-1-1 split: NIV "for", NLT "to control", ESV "contrary to")
• Semicolon preserves the parallel pain-clauses per FHB-9.4-2

verses.fhb_text: To Adam he said, "Because you listened to your wife and ate from the tree about which I commanded you, 'You must not eat from it,' cursed is the ground because of you; through painful toil you will eat from it all the days of your life."
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Judgment on Adam — the ground, his place of dominion, is now cursed.

Renderings:
• "Adam" for ul'adam (no article) — proper name per FHB-9.11-1
• "you listened to your wife" — drops "the voice of" calque per FHB-7.12-1
• "you must not" replaces archaic "thou shalt not" — modern modal per FHB-9.10-1

verses.fhb_text: It will produce thorns and thistles for you, and you will eat the plants of the field.
verses.decision_notes: Simple judgment continuation — ground bears thorns; humanity eats wild growth.

verses.fhb_text: By the sweat of your brow you will eat your food until you return to the ground, since from it you were taken; for you are dust, and to dust you will return.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Death pronounced — the dust-to-dust return.

Renderings:
• "food" for lechem — NIV/NLT consensus (2-1) per FHB-8.4-1
• Semicolons would over-fragment — periods preserve the discrete weight of each clause

verses.fhb_text: Adam named his wife Eve, because she would become the mother of all the living.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Adam names his wife — the naming moment.

Renderings:
• "Eve" for chavvah — proper name introduced at the source's moment of naming per FHB-9.11-1
• "the mother of all the living" — NIV-style modern English

verses.fhb_text: Our Father made garments of skin for Adam and his wife, and clothed them.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The Father's first sacrificial covering — he clothes his children's shame himself.

Renderings:
• "Our Father" for YHWH Elohim — per FHB-1.2-1
• "Adam" for le'adam (no article) — proper name per FHB-9.11-1

verses.fhb_text: Then our Father said, "The man has now become like one of us, knowing good and evil. He must not be allowed to reach out his hand and take also from the tree of life and eat, and live forever."
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Divine plural deliberation — the Trinitarian voice preserved at the moment of expulsion.

Renderings:
• "our Father" for YHWH Elohim — per FHB-1.2-1
• "like one of us" — first-person divine plural preserved per FHB-6.4.1-1(a) (Father, Son, and Holy Spirit in deliberation)
• "the man" for ha'adam (with article) — cosmic-representative use of the article, parallel to v.24
• "Behold" dropped — modern phrasing per FHB-9.10-1

Hebrew/Greek: hen ha'adam (behold the man) → "The man has now become"

verses.fhb_text: So our Father sent him out from the garden of Eden to work the ground from which he had been taken.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Expulsion — the Father sends humanity into exile, but leaves the way to redemption open.

Renderings:
• "our Father" for YHWH Elohim — per FHB-1.2-1

verses.fhb_text: After he drove the man out, he placed cherubim on the east side of the garden of Eden, and a flaming sword flashing back and forth to guard the way to the tree of life.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Cherubim guard the way back — but the way is guarded, not closed forever.

Renderings:
• "the man" for ha'adam (with article) — cosmic-representative use parallel to v.22
• "flaming sword flashing back and forth" for lahat hacherev hammithappechet — NIV-style modern English per FHB-8.4-1

chapter_notes: Genesis 3 is the Fall narrative — the foundational rupture between the Father and his children. FHB applies four governing patterns throughout the chapter: (1) The narrator's YHWH Elohim resolves to "our Father" per FHB-1.2-1 in vv.8, 9, 13, 14, 21, 22, 23 — the Father remains Father even in judgment. (2) The speaker-context exception (FHB-1.2-1) governs vv.1, 3, and 5: the serpent's Elohim and Eve's quoted Elohim render as "God," not "our Father," because non-divine speakers using deity terms generically/predicatively must not collapse the very speaker-distinction the Hebrew preserves — and FHB-2.9-1 anchors the entire canon's counter-lie marker on this distinction. (3) The divine plural at v.22 ("like one of us") preserves first-person divine speech per FHB-6.4.1-1(a), surfacing the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit in deliberation. (4) Adam is treated as established proper name throughout (per FHB-9.11-1, introduced at Gen 2:20); Eve receives her name at v.20 at the Hebrew's moment of naming. One footnote anchors v.1 with the new category "serpents_lie" — establishing the FHB-2.9-1 framing that runs through the rest of Scripture.
